title: Standing with Moms Act
plain_summary: The Standing with Moms Act requires the Department of Health and Human Services to create a website called life.gov and an online tool to help pregnant women find resources and support. The website will list services like healthcare, money help, mental health support, and childcare from government and private groups. The online tool will ask women questions about where they live and give them information about services in their area, including information about abortion risks. The bill does not allow any resources from groups that perform abortions or support abortion to be included on the website. States must help recommend resources for the website, and the government will report on how many people use it.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Standing with Moms Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Awareness for Expecting Mothers
sections.text: This section adds a new title to the Public Health Service Act called Awareness for Expecting Mothers. The Secretary of Health and Human Services must create a website called life.gov within one year. This website will list resources available to pregnant women from federal, state, local, and private groups. The website must include mental health counseling, pregnancy counseling, and services before and after birth. It must provide complete information about alternatives to abortion. It must give information about abortion risks, problems, and failures. It must also link to information about how children develop from the moment of conception. The Secretary must also create a portal on the Health and Human Services website within one year. This portal will ask users questions to give them specific information based on what they are looking for. It will show federal, state, local, and private resources available in their ZIP code area. It will explain risks related to abortion at all stages of pregnancy development. Users can give feedback about how helpful and easy to use the portal was. The portal must include eight types of resources. First, mentorship opportunities including pregnancy help and case management. Second, health and well-being services like women's medical care, obstetrical and gynecological support, abortion pill reversal, breastfeeding help, general health services, primary care, and dental care. Third, financial help, work opportunities, nutrition assistance, childcare, and education opportunities. Fourth, material or legal support including transportation, food, nutrition, clothing, household goods, baby supplies, housing, shelters, maternity homes, tax preparation, legal support for child support, family leave, breastfeeding protections, and custody issues. Fifth, recovery and mental health services including addiction or suicide help, intimate partner violence support, sexual assault help, rape support, sex trafficking services, and counseling for women and families who lose a child unexpectedly. Sixth, prenatal diagnostic services including disability support organizations, medical help for babies, perinatal hospice resources, pregnancy and infant loss support, and literature on pregnancy wellness. Seventh, healing and support services for abortion survivors and their families. Eighth, services that care for children including family planning education, adoption, foster care, and short-term care resources. The Secretary cannot delegate running the portal to anyone below the Office of the Secretary level. The Secretary must develop a plan that includes three parts. First, the portal must have a way for users to take an assessment and agree to let the government use their contact information. Second, the Secretary must reach out by phone or email to follow up with portal users about additional helpful resources. Third, when a user requests specific information after learning about additional resources through the portal, Health and Human Services agents must try their best to give specific information to that user by working with federal, state, local, and private health care providers and resources. Each state must recommend state, local, and private resources to include in the portal based on criteria the Secretary develops. The Secretary will create criteria to decide if recommended resources can appear in the portal. The criteria must require that the resource provider is not a prohibited entity and has been providing services for at least three years in a row. The Secretary may give grants to states to create or support a system that gathers the resources described above according to the criteria. A statewide, regional, or community-based public entity or private nonprofit may coordinate this system if the state thinks it is appropriate. To get a grant, a state must submit an application at the time, in the way, and with the information the Secretary requires. This must include a plan for outreach and awareness activities and a list of service providers that would be included in the state system supported by the grant. The Secretary must make sure the Maternal Mental Health Hotline of the Health Resources and Services Administration does three things. First, it must share information about and links to the life.gov website and portal. Second, it must be able to help families in every state and community in the country. Third, it must include live chat features available twenty-four hours a day to connect people to the information the portal hosts. The resources listed on the life.gov website and made available through the portal and hotline cannot include any resource offered by a prohibited entity. The life.gov website and hotline must make sure families who speak languages other than English have the widest possible access to services. Within one hundred eighty days after the life.gov website and portal are created, the Secretary must submit a report to Congress. This report must include four things. First, how much traffic the website and interactive portal received. Second, user feedback on how accessible and helpful the website and interactive portal were in meeting user needs. Third, insights on gaps in federal, state, local, and private programming for services for pregnant and postpartum women. Fourth, suggestions on how to improve user experience and accessibility based on user feedback and missing resources that would be helpful to include in future updates. The report cannot include any personal identifying information about people who used the website or portal. This section defines four terms. First, abortion means using or prescribing any instrument, medicine, drug, or other substance or device to intentionally kill the unborn child of a woman known to be pregnant or prematurely end the pregnancy of a woman known to be pregnant with an intention other than to increase the probability of a live birth or preserve the life or health of the child after live birth or to remove a dead unborn child. Second, born alive has the same meaning as in section eight part b of title one of the United States Code. Third, prohibited entity means an entity including its affiliates, subsidiaries, successors, and clinics that performs, induces, refers for, or counsels in favor of abortions or provides financial support to any other organization that conducts such activities. Fourth, unborn child means an individual organism of the species homo sapiens beginning at fertilization until the point of being born alive.
